content.problemStatement:
Our well known coding mafia went to the city. Farzi Coder is the boss here. He hired a new member named Lambu Keyboard in his gang. Farzi Coder is so smart (at least that's what he thinks), so he wants to test Lambu Keyboard's programming skills. He gave him this task:

There are `n` buildings (numbered from left to right) with heights H1,H2,…,HnH_1, H_2, \dots, H_nH1​,H2​,…,Hn​. You can start jumping from any building.

The rules of jumping are:


You can only jump to a building with lesser height than the height of the current building.
In the first turn, you can jump either way, and the 2nd jump will be in the opposite direction of the previous jump and must end between the current index and the previous index. For example, if we start jumping from building 5, initially we can jump in either direction. Let's say we jump to building 8, then in the next jump, we can only jump in the left direction and only on buildings with indices 6, 7. Let's say we jump to building 6, then next time we can only jump in the right direction and between 6 and 8, i.e., to 7.
We need to find the maximum number of jumps he can make, considering every building as the starting point.


Input Format

First line contains an integer `n` i.e. the number of buildings.
Second line contains `n` space-separated integers denoting heights of buildings from 1 to `n`.


Output Format
One line containing `n` integers, the ith integer will denote the maximum possible number of jumps starting from the ith building.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer n, the number of buildings. The second line contains n space-separated integers denoting the heights of the buildings from 1 to n.
content.outputFormat: A single line containing n integers, where the ith integer denotes the maximum possible number of jumps starting from the ith building.

sampleTestCases.explanation: If the starting building is with height 1, then you can't jump to any other building. If the starting building is with height 3, then you can jump either to the building with height 2 on the right or with height 1 on the left. But there will be at most one jump. If the starting building is with height 5, then one possible optimal sequence is to jump to the building with height 3, then to the building with height 2. So the maximum number of jumps is 2.

If the starting building is with height 1, then you can't jump to any other building. If the starting building is with height 2, then you can jump to the building with height 1 on the left. If the starting building is with height 3, then you can't jump to any other building.

If the starting building is with height 4, then one possible optimal sequence is to jump to the building with height 3, then to the building with height 2, then to the building with height 1. So the maximum number of jumps is 3. If the starting building is with height 3, then one possible optimal sequence is to jump to the building with height 2, then to the building with height 1. So the maximum number of jumps is 2. If the starting building is with height 2, then one possible optimal sequence is to jump to the building with height 1. So the maximum number of jumps is 1. If the starting building is with height 1, then you can't jump to any other building.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use dynamic programming. We will create two arrays, left and right, where left[i] will store the maximum number of jumps we can make starting from the ith building and moving to the left, and right[i] will store the maximum number of jumps we can make starting from the ith building and moving to the right. We will then use these arrays to calculate the maximum number of jumps we can make starting from each building.
solution.timeComplexity: O(n^2) — because for each building, we are checking all other buildings to the left and right.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(n) — because we are using two arrays of size n to store the maximum number of jumps to the left and right.
